data.title: Netanyahu skeptical about Trump's talks with Iran, but says 'good deal' possible
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday said he is skeptical that a quality deal can be reached with Iran that neuters its threats to the region, but put support behind President Trump’s efforts to reach an agreement. 

Netanyahu made the remarks following a meeting with Trump at the White House on Wednesday. After that meeting, Trump said “nothing definitive” reached in his conversations with Netanyahu, and the two did not do a joint press conference.

Netanyahu on Thursday said his talks with Trump were “primarily” focused on Iran, and described a “very close, very genuine and very open relationship” with the U.S. president.

Netanyahu said he told Trump he’s skeptical of “the quality of an agreement with Iran,” but said Trump is creating “conditions for achieving a good deal.” 

“The President believes that the Iranians have already learned who they are dealing with,” Netanyahu said.

In June, Trump authorized strikes following a White House deadline for Iran to come to an agreement with the U.S.  The U.S. and Israel coordinated strikes on Iran’s air defenses, military sites and nuclear facilities.

In recent weeks, Trump has dispatched additional military assets to the Middle East, including the U.S.S. Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group, and floated sending a second strike-group, as the U.S. and Iran have resumed negotiations over its nuclear program. 

“I think that the conditions he is creating, combined with the fact that they surely understand they made a mistake last time by not reaching an agreement, could lead to the conditions for achieving a good deal,” Netanyahu said. 

“He wanted to hear my opinion. I won’t hide from you that I expressed general skepticism about the quality of any agreement with Iran,” he continued. 

“But I said that if an agreement is indeed reached, it must include elements that are very important to us, to Israel — and, in my opinion, not only to Israel.”

Israel’s red-lines are no Iranian nuclear enrichment, ending its ballistic missile program and ending Iran’s support for terrorist proxies.

Iranian officials have said they are willing to discuss their nuclear activities but claimed that uranium enrichment – the fuel for a nuclear weapon – is a national right. Iran claims its nuclear program is for peaceful purposes but has stockpiled enough enriched uranium to quickly create a bomb. 

In 2018, Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Obama-era nuclear deal with Iran, the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, which imposed limits on Iran’s nuclear enrichment in exchange for sanctions relief.

data.title: Trump nominates hospitality executive to lead National Park Service
data.description:
President Trump is nominating a food service and hospitality executive to lead the National Park Service (NPS).

Trump’s pick to lead the U.S.’s national parks, Scott Socha, is Delaware North’s president of parks and resorts, as well as president of Delaware North Australia.

Delaware North has contracted with the NPS.

According to Delaware North’s website, its parks and resorts subsidiary provides services in seven national parks and operates lodging in five communities near parks including near Yosemite.

Socha has been with the company since 1999.

His appointment requires Senate confirmation.  

Trump also announced he would nominate Kevin Lilly, who worked in wealth management prior to reportedly joining the Trump administration, to lead the Fish and Wildlife Service.

data.title: Watch live: Trump, Zeldin expected to announce repeal of climate endangerment finding
data.description:
President Trump and Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin are set to announce plans Thursday to repeal the landmark 2009 legal finding that climate change poses a threat to the public.

The move marks a significant escalation in the administration’s fight against green policies and its efforts to deny climate change. The legal finding, known as the endangerment finding, underpins the nation’s climate rules.

It also comes a day after Trump directed the Pentagon to purchase more power from coal plants in a boost for the declining fossil fuel.

The event is scheduled to begin at 1:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Judge blocks Hegseth, Pentagon bid to punish Mark Kelly over 'illegal orders' video
data.description:
A federal judge on Thursday blocked the Pentagon’s efforts to censure Sen. Mark Kelly and lower his retirement rank in the wake of a video in which the Arizona senator and other Democrats called on service members to reject unlawful orders.    

U.S. District Judge Richard Leon, an appointee of former President George W. Bush, noted the military principal that servicemembers enjoy weakened First Amendment protections to preserve discipline in the armed forces, but said that no court had ever extended that doctrine to retired service members.  

He said he would not be the first to do so. 

“This Court has all it needs to conclude that Defendants have trampled on Senator Kelly’s First Amendment freedoms and threatened the constitutional liberties of millions of military retirees,” the judge wrote.  

Leon quoted Bob Dylan to say, “You don’t need a weatherman to know which way the wind blows.” 

“To say the least, our retired veterans deserve more respect from their Government,” the judge added, “and our Constitution demands they receive it!”  

Kelly, a retired Navy captain, sued last month after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced the Pentagon had initiated retirement grade determination proceedings and issued a formal letter of censure over the November video.  

Five other Democrats with either intelligence or military backgrounds joined Kelly in calling on troops to “refuse illegal orders”: Sen. Elissa Slotkin (Mich.) and Reps. Chris Deluzio (Pa.), Maggie Goodlander (N.H.), Chrissy Houlahan (Pa.) and Jason Crow (Colo.). 

At a hearing last week, Kelly’s attorneys argued that the Department of Defense sought to inflict “unprecedented punishment” on the senator for his protected speech. They warned that the consequences of a ruling against Kelly could have ramifications beyond the senator.  

“Their rule would extend to every single retired service member in the country,” said lawyer Benjamin Mizer. 

The Justice Department said that the speech of veterans is more limited than that of regular civilians, contending that retirement from active service is not the same as separation from the military.  

DOJ lawyer John Bailey urged Kelly to see the Pentagon’s process through before involving the courts and warned that, without deference to the military, Leon risked undermining the armed forces and its internal processes.  

“That would send a message that the military does not have control of its own forces,” Bailey said.   

At a press conference Wednesday, Kelly called the probe “straight from the authoritarian playbook” but suggested it’s a fight that’s bigger than him alone.  

“I want to be clear about something: This is not a ‘good-news’ story,” Kelly said at a press conference Wednesday. “This is a story about how hard Donald Trump and his cronies are trying to break our system in order to silence anyone who lawfully speaks out against them. 

“And to send a signal to every American that they better think twice before they speak out, or else they might be next,” he added.  

DEVELOPING

data.title: How to raise birth rates is the wrong question: Here's what we should be asking
data.description:
As a demographer who studies American childbearing decision-making and behaviors, I’m increasingly being pulled into the near-constant discourse on how to raise birth rates, spurred by the record low total fertility rate of 1.6 births per woman the U.S. reached in 2024. 

Conservative groups, like the Heritage Foundation, have been some of the loudest voices, putting forth policies they believe will raise birth rates by encouraging heterosexual marriage and discouraging divorce, restricting access to social welfare programs and — perhaps counterintuitively — limiting access to IVF. On the other side of the spectrum, some argue that leftists and feminists should present their own progressive solutions to raise birth rates, like child care or paid leave.

Regardless of where they’re coming from, pronatalist efforts ignore a truth from demographic research: Policies to raise birth rates do not result in substantial or sustained increases.  

Declining birth rates have real implications for population size and composition, the labor market and economic growth, tax revenue and programs funded by taxes, such as Social Security, and local communities. But attempts to raise birth rates are rarely successful. That’s the conclusion of a recent review of decades of other countries’ family policies that were either explicitly designed to raise birth rates or could have done so indirectly, by reducing the costs or challenges associated with childrearing.

The review considered three specific types of policies: cash benefits (“baby bonuses,” family allowances, tax relief), parental leave and child care support. The authors conclude that although policy — primarily paid parental leave and a robust child care infrastructure — can raise birth rates, such efforts have only modest effects, raising the number of children per woman by between 0.1 and 0.2. That is hardly enough to push the U.S. total fertility rate above the well-known (if misunderstood) “replacement-level fertility” threshold of 2.1.

Efforts to raise birth rates by restricting access to abortion don’t work either. Although abortion bans can produce short-term increases in birth rates, they ultimately lower the number of children women have over their lifetime, along with increasing the risk of negative health outcomes for mothers and infants and a host of other social ills.

Asking what can be done to raise birth rates, then, is the wrong question. Demographic research consistently shows that birth rates generally move in one direction: They decline over time. Further, population targets that focus on childbearing often ignore women’s reproductive autonomy, leading to coercion and risks to women’s health.

A better question is: Can we do more to make parenthood easier for people who want to have children? Certainly, many people are unable to have as many children as they would like, and policies such as affordable, accessible child care and paid family leave might help people reach their personal family goals. Child care and paid leave also improve children’s outcomes, support parents’ mental and physical health and contribute to a stronger, more productive labor force. 

We should continue to welcome new initiatives, like the child-care expansions recently announced in New York. Similarly, expanding access to infertility treatment could help people achieve their own childbearing goals and boost birth rates slightly. But we should support these things regardless of whether they result in sizable increases in birth rates over the long term.  

A second critical question is how do we support the population that we currently, and will likely, have in the future, given current trends? Chasing economic growth by focusing on indefinite population growth, achieved primarily through more births, is a Ponzi scheme. As with other high-income countries, low birth rates are likely to be a persistent reality for the U.S., but a 2024 economic review piece notes that “a thoughtful and consistent policy response can mitigate most of the adverse consequences” of low fertility. Others have suggested that low fertility — with proper planning — can even be beneficial. 

The constant emphasis on raising birth rates deflects attention away from levers that would be more effective in addressing the broader consequences of low fertility, such as immigration policy that welcomes immigrants as an integral part of the U.S. economy. It takes discussions about how we structure and fund Social Security off the table and sidelines conversations about accommodating older workers and building infrastructure to address the health needs of an aging population.

It’s worth noting, too, that children born today would not immediately contribute to the labor market or produce tax revenue but would actually be net economic burdens for two decades.  

It’s not that low birth rates don’t present real challenges. It’s that the solution to those challenges isn’t to try to raise birth rates. We need to shift the conversation, asking ourselves how we can develop a sustainable response to demographic change by adapting and investing in the populations and institutions we have rather than chasing unattainable population targets.

Karen Benjamin Guzzo is director of the Carolina Population Center and a professor in the Department of Sociology at University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.

data.title: Nancy Mace on unredacted names in Epstein files: 'You would be shocked'
data.description:
Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) on Wednesday said people would be “shocked” by the names that have been redacted from files tied to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein that were publicly released on Jan. 30.

Her comments come after lawmakers were given the opportunity to review the unredacted files in a reading room at the Department of Justice.

“You would be shocked, Piers, at some of the names that I have seen that came across the DOJ in emails that the DOJ is protecting,” Mace said during a Wednesday appearance on “Piers Morgan Uncensored.”

“And we’re talking about people on both sides of the aisle. We’re talking about famous people, rich people, people in power, prime ministers, former prime ministers, former presidents, etcetera, media personalities that are named in these files,” she added.

Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) shared the names of the six men he claimed were “likely incriminated” in the Epstein files on the House floor Tuesday.

Mace has been a vocal backer of Epstein’s survivors, who are pushing to publicize the names of wealthy and powerful men associated with the disgraced financier’s crimes. 

Mace, who’s spoken publicly about her experience as a sexual assault survivor, left a meeting with Epstein victims in September visibly upset.

“I feel the immense pain of how hard all victims are fighting for themselves because we know absolutely no one will fight for us,” she wrote on X at the time.

Mace is among a group of GOP lawmakers who have demanded more transparency from the Trump administration, joining Democrats to pass the Epstein Files Transparency Act late last year.

Some of their colleagues in the upper chamber say they are just now realizing the weight of crimes committed by the late millionaire.

“I’ve not been one of the members who has glommed on to this as an issue,” Republican Sen. Cynthia Lummis (Wyo.) said earlier this week. “I’ve sort of intentionally deferred to others to find out about it. But 9-year-old victims … wow.”

Lummis added that “initially, my reaction to all this was, ‘I don’t care. I don’t know what the big deal is.’ But now I see what the big deal is, and it was worth investigating. And the members of Congress that have been pushing this were not wrong. So that’s really my only reaction.”

Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) — who said in early January “I don’t give a rip about Epstein,” as reported by Politico — has changed her tune since reviewing the unredacted files.

“Terrifying language in the Epstein Files I viewed yesterday,” Boebert wrote in a Wednesday post on X.

“-Emails about torture -Frequent talk of ‘consumption’? -A restaurant called ‘The Cannibal’ The women around Epstein were also deeply involved in trafficking children. These are sick, sick people.”

data.title: Judge Boasberg orders return of Venezuelan men deported under Alien Enemies Act
data.description:
A federal judge on Thursday ordered the Trump administration to facilitate the return of some of the Venezuelan men it sent to an El Salvador prison last year by invoking the Alien Enemies Act. 

U.S. District Judge James Boasberg ruled the government must pay to fly back or accept at a port of entry any of the men who are now in countries other than Venezuela. Upon arrival in the U.S., they will be detained as they fight accusations they are Tren de Aragua gang members. 

The judge previously ruled the government violated the due process rights of the 137 Venezuelan men, and they were entitled to a hearing. But Boasberg had left the door open for a legal process that didn’t entail their physical return.

“Apparently not interested in participating in this process, the Government’s responses essentially told the Court to pound sand,” Boasberg wrote in his new decision. 

Boasberg has sparred with the Justice Department ever since he ordered the March flights to turn around. President Trump responded by demanding the judge’s impeachment, while Boasberg has long pushed to hold the administration in contempt. 

“Mindful of the flagrancy of the Government’s violations of the deportees’ due-process rights that landed Plaintiffs in this situation, the Court refuses to let them languish in the solution-less mire Defendants propose,” Boasberg wrote. 

His order demands the government submit a list within 15 days of individuals who wish to return to the U.S. Two weeks later, the government must send him an update explaining how and when it will transport them. 

Trump first invoked the Alien Enemies Act last March to swiftly deport alleged Venezuelan gang members as part of his aggressive immigration crackdown.

The 1798 law enables migrants to be summarily removed amid an “invasion” or “predatory incursion” by a foreign nation. It has only been used three previous times, all during declared wars. 

Whether Tren de Aragua’s activities enable Trump to invoke the law is still working its way through the courts. The 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals heard oral arguments in the leading case last month and is now drafting a decision, which could next be appealed to the Supreme Court. 

Boasberg, meanwhile, oversees the case involving the 137 Venezuelan men initially deported under the law on several deportation flights last March.  

They were sent to a notorious megaprison in El Salvador known as the Terrorism Confinement Center and later released in a prisoner swap with Venezuela. 

Boasberg’s order notably does not apply to any of the migrants physically in Venezuela, given the country’s volatile political situation in the wake of U.S. forces deposing President Nicolás Maduro.

“Once again, at this point, we are not talking about a substantial number of people —
particularly because the Court, mindful of the previously mentioned foreign affairs concern, does not extend this requirement to deportees remaining in Venezuela,” Boasberg wrote Thursday.

data.title: DOJ antitrust head steps down
data.description:
Gail Slater, the head of the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) antitrust division, announced Thursday that she is stepping down.

“It is with great sadness and abiding hope that I leave my role as AAG for Antitrust today,” she wrote in a post on the social platform X. “It was indeed the honor of a lifetime to serve in this role. Huge thanks to all who supported me this past year, most especially the men and women of [the antitrust division].”

Slater, who previously served as an adviser to Vice President Vance, was tasked with overseeing several major antitrust cases against Big Tech firms, including Google and Apple.

She leaves after a brief tenure at the agency that was marked by tensions with top Trump officials. Two of her deputies were fired last year after an internal dispute over the handling of a merger between Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) and Juniper Networks.

One of these deputies, Roger Alford, slammed DOJ leadership outside the antitrust division shortly after his ouster for allegedly allowing politically connected lobbyists to influence decisionmaking.

In recent weeks, new tensions had reportedly arisen between Slater and DOJ leadership. According to Semafor, Slater attempted to let go of her current chief of staff but was overruled by Attorney General Pam Bondi.

data.title: Iowa lawmakers introduce measure hoping to attract Chicago Bears to state
data.description:
The Chicago Bears, playing in Iowa? A bill introduced this week in the Hawkeye State’s Senate is attempting to make that a reality.

The legislation, introduced by Republican state Sens. Dan Dawson, Scott Webster, Mike Bousselot, Dawn Driscoll, Carrie Koelker, Kerry Gruenhagen, and Cherielynn Westrich, calls for modifying Iowa’s major economic growth attraction program to “include incentivizing” the building of a professional sports stadium by an NFL franchise in the state.

The legislation calls for expanding the economic growth program, which provides incentives to businesses that invest at least $1 billion in Iowa and are “primarily engaged in advanced manufacturing, biosciences, or research and development,” to include an NFL franchise. The incentives include sales and use tax refunds, qualifying investment tax credits and withholding tax credits. 

This comes as the Bears, who have played at Soldier Field in downtown Chicago for over five decades, continue their search for a new venue. While the franchise previously settled on a site in Arlington Heights, Ill. — roughly 25 miles northwest of the Windy City — Bears CEO Kevin Warren said in December that state leadership has not prioritized that project.

“Consequently, in addition to Arlington Park, we need to expand our search and critically evaluate opportunities throughout the wider Chicagoland region, including Northwest Indiana,
Warren wrote in an open letter to the fanbase.

That prompted lawmakers in the Hoosier State to propose that the Bears move to nearby Gary, Ind., which is roughly 30 miles southeast of Chicago along Lake Michigan.

Now, there is another suitor for one of the original NFL franchises. 

“While Illinois and Indiana squabble over this issue, we are ready to get off the sidelines and into the game,” Gruenhagen said in a statement, according to the Des Moines Register. “Bringing an NFL team to Iowa would attract jobs, tourism and fans to our state and give us the opportunity to showcase what Iowa really has to offer. 

“Iowans have dedicated themselves to our college sports teams, and we’re ready to attract a professional team to our great state.”

data.title: Nick Shirley's independent journalism is the future of news
data.description:
It is hard to believe that a young independent journalist could produce content that would lead to weeks of news coverage and dramatically change the conversation in both the social media and traditional media worlds. But Nick Shirley has done it. Beyond the stories Shirley has uncovered, his reporting provides a glimpse into how journalism may be conducted in the years ahead. 

Shirley’s reporting about possible fraud in Minnesota’s day care industry helped initiate the flood of federal immigration officers into the state. That in turn led to days of demonstrations, the shootings of two protesters, and fraud investigations. It also led to Minnesota politicians such as Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Mayor Jacob Frey (D) turning up the rhetorical heat against federal law enforcement, sounding much like 1861 Confederates in the process.

Trump administration officials have also engaged in fiery speech, further exacerbating the confrontation. And now leading congressional Democrats are threatening to cut funding for the Department of Homeland Security because of perceived overreach by ICE enforcement officers.

All of this news because of one enterprising, independent storyteller who was unafraid to take on a story that touches on the multiple flashpoints of immigration, ethnicity and government ineffectiveness.

Shirley’s reporting has undoubtedly uncovered important information about fraudulent use of taxpayer money. It has also exposed the inability or unwillingness of politicians and government bureaucrats to prevent or even stop the abuse. 

Shirley’s disclosures have sparked sociopolitical conflict that is uncomfortable for the nation to monitor, forcing to the surface debates about immigration enforcement, citizen protests, and federal versus state jurisdiction. This has made for real news — exactly the kind of watchdog news expected when the nation’s framers created a free and independent press.  

Shirley, at just 23, would seem an unlikely person to change the course of the nation’s news agenda. He has no apparent training in journalism. His formal education ended at his high school graduation. His venture in Minnesota was not his first endeavor into activist journalism; he has been on the scene at protests in Portland, drug dealing in New York City, and various other ICE operations. Shirley even participated in a White House roundtable last fall discussing antifa.

But Shirley’s work in Minnesota generated the most traction, demonstrating the increasing power of social media journalists. For his efforts, he is now the target of threats and has had to hire security to protect himself.

He has also been criticized in some established media circles for his lack of credentials and whether he is qualified to engage in real journalism. The First Amendment, however, allows all Americans essentially to be journalists. The free press right is not confined to journalism school graduates or traditional reporters with official press cards. Any citizen can contribute to the public sphere of information, which Shirley and countless other contributors are now doing through various digital means.

In fact, a report by the Poynter institute indicates that one-third of America’s journalists are now considered “creator journalists,” posting self-publishing content independent of established news outlets.

These self-publishing content providers may lack brand names, but they bring an independence that would be difficult to muster working under the direction of media executives in corporate towers. Providers such as Shirley are on the ground interacting with real people and, indeed, changing the trajectory of the nation’s news. That independent streak resembles America’s early days when patriots, such as Samuel Adams and Thomas Paine bypassed colonial authorities’ pressures to fuel America’s dialogue for independence.  

Establishment media outlets no doubt wring their hands about the increasing toehold of independent journalism creators. But the corporate media establishment should consider its own missteps that prompted citizens to leave the news audience, thus opening the door for alternative information sources. A Gallup survey late last year showed Americans’ trust in traditional news outlets has dipped to a new low of 28 percent.  

The influx of independent journalists such as Shirley will certainly add to what is already a sort of “wild west” in today’s reporting landscape. News consumers will have to up their media literacy game to carefully scrutinize news content and sources. The expansion of the reportorial terrain, though, is perhaps what America needs in what is becoming a “post-news” era, so people such as Shirley can broaden a news agenda that once was the purview of only mainstream news outlets. 

Jeffrey M. McCall is a media critic and professor of communication at DePauw University. He has worked as a radio news director, a newspaper reporter and as a political media consultant.
